limitation: All abstracts in the current export are embargoed_or_pending, so this narrative uses title-level abstract signals and agenda metadata.
disclaimer: ASCO Hype is AI-generated conference commentary for informational and entertainment purposes only. It is not medical, clinical, scientific, legal, or financial advice. Always consult qualified professionals and primary sources.

narratives.label: Friday, May 29
narratives.headline: Friday, May 29: Pediatric Oncology leads the day, with Medical Education and Professional Development close behind
narratives.oneLine: 24 agenda sessions, 67 oral abstract presentations, and 67 total timed abstract presentations shape the day, while 7295 embargoed abstract records provide title-level watch signals until full text is released.
broadcastArc: Open with the room map: 24 sessions are scheduled, and the leading tracks are Pediatric Oncology, Medical Education and Professional Development, Care Delivery/Models of Care, Hematologic Malignancies—Lymphoma and Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia.
Use the oral abstract sessions as the evidence spine for the day, with education and special sessions providing context between data-heavy blocks.
Keep the poster wall callout ready for the next Hall A Posters and Exhibits block.

thoracic oncology sets up the high-attention evidence stream, with metastatic non-small cell disease and local-regional or small-cell sessions giving the channel a recurring clinical-science storyline
developmental therapeutics keeps the discovery lane active, especially targeted therapy, immunotherapy, tumor biology, and early-phase strategy
Close the day with a media-monitor reset: reviewed media and broadcast signals can interrupt the schedule spine, but they must be attributed and clearly labeled.

tentpoleSessions.overview: As increasingly complex immunotherapy combinations are incorporated into cancer treatment, e.g. immunotherapy-targeted therapy, and new modalities are developed, e.g. personalized vaccines and tumor antigen-directed cell therapies, questions have surfaced related to emerging resistance, sequencing and combining therapy, and addressing adverse events.

• When Representation Becomes Survival: Changing My Perspective on Clinical Trials
• Finding Your Voice Through Illness
• Healing Beyond the Scan
• The Bridge of Beams: Connecting the Divide in Cancer Care
• Borrowed Courage: How Others' Faith Sparks Our Boldest Moves
tentpoleSessions.overview: ASCO Voices is an opportunity for members of the oncology community to share a personal story or experience on a global stage. The session provides a unique platform to use your voice to support and inspire fellow oncology professionals. 5 auditioners are selected to give a 7-minute lightning talk during this session—no podium, no slides; just a story and a stage.

narratives.hostOpen: Welcome to ASCO Hype for Friday, May 29. Turn it up: this is the live conference desk, radio-DJ style, source-forward and moving fast. Today the agenda leans into Pediatric Oncology, with Medical Education and Professional Development also carrying major attention. We will use the official agenda as the spine, title-level abstract signals as watch points, and reviewed audience or media inputs as interruptions. Poster wall callout, W-poster watch: Hall A - Posters and Exhibits is the floor to watch. If a poster wall stop is buzzing, say the hall twice, ask listeners to verify the location in the ASCO app and on-site signage, and invite #ASCOHype posts from attendees on the ground. Audience fuel check: if you find the best snacks or coffee in the Exhibitor Hall, tag #ASCOHype on X and tell the desk where you found it. ASCO Hype will treat these as audience recommendations, not endorsements.
narratives.hostClose: That is the Friday, May 29 arc: follow the official schedule, watch the high-density tracks, and treat every abstract-title signal as provisional until primary sources and full text are available. Keep tagging #ASCOHype on X with the best snacks, coffee, poster-wall hits, and media/broadcast tips you find so the desk can review audience signals. ASCO Hype is AI-generated conference commentary for informational and entertainment purposes only. It is not medical, clinical, scientific, legal, or financial advice. Always consult qualified professionals and primary sources.

narratives.label: Saturday, May 30
narratives.headline: Saturday, May 30: Lung Cancer—Non-Small Cell Metastatic leads the day, with Special Sessions close behind
narratives.oneLine: 46 agenda sessions, 104 oral abstract presentations, and 118 total timed abstract presentations shape the day, while 7295 embargoed abstract records provide title-level watch signals until full text is released.
broadcastArc: Open with the room map: 46 sessions are scheduled, and the leading tracks are Lung Cancer—Non-Small Cell Metastatic, Special Sessions, Melanoma/Skin Cancers, Gynecologic Cancer.
Use the oral abstract sessions as the evidence spine for the day, with education and special sessions providing context between data-heavy blocks.
Run poster wall and W-poster watch callouts for Hall A - Posters and Exhibits, especially when a poster session block opens.

thoracic oncology sets up the high-attention evidence stream, with metastatic non-small cell disease and local-regional or small-cell sessions giving the channel a recurring clinical-science storyline
GI oncology supplies a dense cross-section of colorectal, gastroesophageal, pancreatic, hepatobiliary, and anal-cancer discussion
Close the day with a media-monitor reset: reviewed media and broadcast signals can interrupt the schedule spine, but they must be attributed and clearly labeled.

tentpoleSessions.title: Pediatric Oncology II
tentpoleSessions.chairs: Raya Saab, MD; Shushan Hovsepyan, MD
tentpoleSessions.speakers: Feifei Sun, MD (Department of Pediatric Oncology, Sun Yat-sen University Cancer Center, State Key Laboratory of Oncology in South China, Collaborative Innovation Center for Cancer Medicine); Sara-Jane N. Onyeama, MD (Cancer and Blood Disease Institute, Children's Hospital Los Angeles; Keck School of Medicine, University of Southern California); Panel Discussion (ASCO); Nilay Shah, MD (Nationwide Children's Hospital); Suzanne J. Forrest, MD (Dana-Farber/Boston Children's Cancer and Blood Disorders Center); Rachel M. Hurley, MD, PhD (Children's Hospital of Philadelphia); Venkatraman Radhakrishnan, MD, DM, MSc, MBBS (Cancer Institute (WIA))
tentpoleSessions.panelists: Sara-Jane N. Onyeama, MD; Feifei Sun, MD; Suzanne J. Forrest, MD; Nilay Shah, MD; Venkatraman Radhakrishnan, MD, DM, MSc, MBBS; Rachel M. Hurley, MD, PhD
tentpoleSessions.topics:
• Alrizomadlin (APG-115) alone or in combination with lisaftoclax (APG-2575) for the treatment of pediatric patients with relapsed/metastatic rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS) or other soft-tissue sarcomas (STSs).
• RAPID: A pilot feasibility study of rapid dinutuximab infusion in patients with relapsed/refractory (RR) high-risk neuroblastoma (HRNBL).
• Panel Question and Answer
• Interim analysis of CaboMain: A prospective, single-arm phase 2 clinical trial of cabozantinib as maintenance therapy for patients with “ultra-high-risk” pediatric solid tumors.
• Phase II trial of olaparib and ceralasertib in patients with recurrent osteosarcoma: Lung-only resectable cohort efficacy and correlative biomarker results.
• Panel Question and Answer
• Characterization and validation of a methylation signature as a prognostic marker in osteosarcoma.
• Dexamethasone-free antiemetic prophylaxis with olanzapine...
tentpoleSessions.overview: Presenters give an overview of their novel research and answer questions during a moderated panel discussion.

tentpoleSessions.overview: The session will begin with a foundational presentation on the session topic to set the stage for the abstracts to follow. Abstract presenters summarize research findings, while discussants place the abstracts in the appropriate context with a focus on how the findings apply to clinical practice. Abstracts in these sessions may span disease sites or disciplines, but they are unified by their focus on a key scientific topic.

posterSessions.topics:
• Cardiovascular disease incidence in women with early-onset breast cancer.
• Machine learning (ML)-based prediction of fatal immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI)–associated myocarditis (MC) with multi-cohort validation.
• Artificial intelligence surrogate models to predict long-term cardiovascular effects of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapies using electrocardiograms.
• The impact of body composition on cardiac function in women with early breast cancer taking cardiotoxic chemotherapy.
• Exercise and cardiovascular risk among breast cancer patients.
• Time-resolved cardiovascular risk after immune checkpoint inhibitors: A large real-world cancer cohort study.
• The genetic polymorphism underlying immune checkpoint inhibitor–induced endocrine toxicity.
• Cardiac safety of L-annamycin at high cumulative anthracycline exposure: Pooled analysis.
• The association between cardiovascular dis...
posterSessions.overview: Presenters outline their research via posters displayed throughout their assigned Session(s). Authors are available to answer questions from attendees regarding the data presented. Electronic posters and supplemental materials are available online beginning on the day of their assigned Poster Session(s).

narratives.hostOpen: Welcome to ASCO Hype for Saturday, May 30. Turn it up: this is the live conference desk, radio-DJ style, source-forward and moving fast. Today the agenda leans into Lung Cancer—Non-Small Cell Metastatic, with Special Sessions also carrying major attention. We will use the official agenda as the spine, title-level abstract signals as watch points, and reviewed audience or media inputs as interruptions. Poster wall callout, W-poster watch: Hall A - Posters and Exhibits is the floor to watch. If a poster wall stop is buzzing, say the hall twice, ask listeners to verify the location in the ASCO app and on-site signage, and invite #ASCOHype posts from attendees on the ground. Audience fuel check: if you find the best snacks or coffee in the Exhibitor Hall, tag #ASCOHype on X and tell the desk where you found it. ASCO Hype will treat these as audience recommendations, not endorsements.
narratives.hostClose: That is the Saturday, May 30 arc: follow the official schedule, watch the high-density tracks, and treat every abstract-title signal as provisional until primary sources and full text are available. Keep tagging #ASCOHype on X with the best snacks, coffee, poster-wall hits, and media/broadcast tips you find so the desk can review audience signals. ASCO Hype is AI-generated conference commentary for informational and entertainment purposes only. It is not medical, clinical, scientific, legal, or financial advice. Always consult qualified professionals and primary sources.

narratives.label: Sunday, May 31
narratives.headline: Sunday, May 31: Special Sessions leads the day, with Central Nervous System Tumors close behind
narratives.oneLine: 43 agenda sessions, 114 oral abstract presentations, and 129 total timed abstract presentations shape the day, while 7295 embargoed abstract records provide title-level watch signals until full text is released.
broadcastArc: Open with the room map: 43 sessions are scheduled, and the leading tracks are Special Sessions, Central Nervous System Tumors, Breast Cancer—Metastatic, Prevention, Risk Reduction, and Genetics.
Make the plenary session the editorial center of gravity for the day, then use oral abstract sessions to build the before-and-after context.
Run poster wall and W-poster watch callouts for Hall A - Posters and Exhibits, especially when a poster session block opens.

thoracic oncology sets up the high-attention evidence stream, with metastatic non-small cell disease and local-regional or small-cell sessions giving the channel a recurring clinical-science storyline
breast cancer anchors patient-centered treatment sequencing, adjuvant strategy, metastatic decision-making, and antibody-drug-conjugate watch points
Close the day with a media-monitor reset: reviewed media and broadcast signals can interrupt the schedule spine, but they must be attributed and clearly labeled.

tentpoleSessions.overview: The session will begin with a foundational presentation on the session topic to set the stage for the abstracts to follow. Abstract presenters summarize research findings, while discussants place the abstracts in the appropriate context with a focus on how the findings apply to clinical practice. Abstracts in these sessions may span disease sites or disciplines, but they are unified by their focus on a key scientific topic.

narratives.hostOpen: Welcome to ASCO Hype for Sunday, May 31. Turn it up: this is the live conference desk, radio-DJ style, source-forward and moving fast. Today the agenda leans into Special Sessions, with Central Nervous System Tumors also carrying major attention. We will use the official agenda as the spine, title-level abstract signals as watch points, and reviewed audience or media inputs as interruptions. Poster wall callout, W-poster watch: Hall A - Posters and Exhibits is the floor to watch. If a poster wall stop is buzzing, say the hall twice, ask listeners to verify the location in the ASCO app and on-site signage, and invite #ASCOHype posts from attendees on the ground. Audience fuel check: if you find the best snacks or coffee in the Exhibitor Hall, tag #ASCOHype on X and tell the desk where you found it. ASCO Hype will treat these as audience recommendations, not endorsements.
narratives.hostClose: That is the Sunday, May 31 arc: follow the official schedule, watch the high-density tracks, and treat every abstract-title signal as provisional until primary sources and full text are available. Keep tagging #ASCOHype on X with the best snacks, coffee, poster-wall hits, and media/broadcast tips you find so the desk can review audience signals. ASCO Hype is AI-generated conference commentary for informational and entertainment purposes only. It is not medical, clinical, scientific, legal, or financial advice. Always consult qualified professionals and primary sources.

narratives.label: Monday, June 1
narratives.headline: Monday, June 1: Special Sessions leads the day, with Head and Neck Cancer close behind
narratives.oneLine: 66 agenda sessions, 89 oral abstract presentations, and 109 total timed abstract presentations shape the day, while 7295 embargoed abstract records provide title-level watch signals until full text is released.
broadcastArc: Open with the room map: 66 sessions are scheduled, and the leading tracks are Special Sessions, Head and Neck Cancer, Gastrointestinal Cancer—Gastroesophageal, Pancreatic, and Hepatobiliary, Prevention, Risk Reduction, and Genetics.
Use the oral abstract sessions as the evidence spine for the day, with education and special sessions providing context between data-heavy blocks.
Run poster wall and W-poster watch callouts for Hall A - Posters and Exhibits, especially when a poster session block opens.
breast cancer anchors patient-centered treatment sequencing, adjuvant strategy, metastatic decision-making, and antibody-drug-conjugate watch points
GI oncology supplies a dense cross-section of colorectal, gastroesophageal, pancreatic, hepatobiliary, and anal-cancer discussion
Close the day with a media-monitor reset: reviewed media and broadcast signals can interrupt the schedule spine, but they must be attributed and clearly labeled.

tentpoleSessions.overview: The session will begin with a foundational presentation on the session topic to set the stage for the abstracts to follow. Abstract presenters summarize research findings, while discussants place the abstracts in the appropriate context with a focus on how the findings apply to clinical practice. Abstracts in these sessions may span disease sites or disciplines, but they are unified by their focus on a key scientific topic.

narratives.hostOpen: Welcome to ASCO Hype for Monday, June 1. Turn it up: this is the live conference desk, radio-DJ style, source-forward and moving fast. Today the agenda leans into Special Sessions, with Head and Neck Cancer also carrying major attention. We will use the official agenda as the spine, title-level abstract signals as watch points, and reviewed audience or media inputs as interruptions. Poster wall callout, W-poster watch: Hall A - Posters and Exhibits is the floor to watch. If a poster wall stop is buzzing, say the hall twice, ask listeners to verify the location in the ASCO app and on-site signage, and invite #ASCOHype posts from attendees on the ground. Audience fuel check: if you find the best snacks or coffee in the Exhibitor Hall, tag #ASCOHype on X and tell the desk where you found it. ASCO Hype will treat these as audience recommendations, not endorsements.
narratives.hostClose: That is the Monday, June 1 arc: follow the official schedule, watch the high-density tracks, and treat every abstract-title signal as provisional until primary sources and full text are available. Keep tagging #ASCOHype on X with the best snacks, coffee, poster-wall hits, and media/broadcast tips you find so the desk can review audience signals. ASCO Hype is AI-generated conference commentary for informational and entertainment purposes only. It is not medical, clinical, scientific, legal, or financial advice. Always consult qualified professionals and primary sources.

narratives.label: Tuesday, June 2
narratives.headline: Tuesday, June 2: Gastrointestinal Cancer—Colorectal and Anal leads the day, with Developmental Therapeutics—Molecularly Targeted Agents and Tumor Biology close behind
narratives.oneLine: 11 agenda sessions, 43 oral abstract presentations, and 43 total timed abstract presentations shape the day, while 7295 embargoed abstract records provide title-level watch signals until full text is released.
broadcastArc: Open with the room map: 11 sessions are scheduled, and the leading tracks are Gastrointestinal Cancer—Colorectal and Anal, Developmental Therapeutics—Molecularly Targeted Agents and Tumor Biology, Prevention, Risk Reduction, and Genetics, Breast Cancer—Metastatic.
Use the oral abstract sessions as the evidence spine for the day, with education and special sessions providing context between data-heavy blocks.
Keep the poster wall callout ready for the next Hall A Posters and Exhibits block.
breast cancer anchors patient-centered treatment sequencing, adjuvant strategy, metastatic decision-making, and antibody-drug-conjugate watch points
GI oncology supplies a dense cross-section of colorectal, gastroesophageal, pancreatic, hepatobiliary, and anal-cancer discussion
Close the day with a media-monitor reset: reviewed media and broadcast signals can interrupt the schedule spine, but they must be attributed and clearly labeled.

tentpoleSessions.title: Navigating Treatment Decisions in Early-Stage Hormone Receptor–Positive Breast Cancer
tentpoleSessions.chairs: Kevin Kalinsky, MD, FASCO, MS
tentpoleSessions.speakers: Erin Frances Cobain, MD (Michigan Medicine); Kevin Kalinsky, MD, FASCO, MS (Winship Cancer Institute of Emory University); Eitan Amir, PhD, MBChB (Princess Margaret Cancer Centre); Panel Discussion (ASCO)
tentpoleSessions.panelists: Erin Frances Cobain, MD; Eitan Amir, PhD, MBChB
tentpoleSessions.topics:
• ctDNA Surveillance in Early Breast Cancer: Handling Requests and Results
• Genomic Scores in Node-Positive Premenopausal Disease: When to Test, How to Act
• Treatment Decision-Making in Locally Advanced, Favorable Biology Breast Cancer
• Panel Question and Answer
tentpoleSessions.overview: Treating patients with early hormone receptor-positive (HR ) breast cancer presents many challenges in clinical decision-making, including when and how to use genomic scores in node-positive premenopausal disease; how to respond to patient requests for ctDNA surveillance and interpret results; and how to incorporate axillary de-escalation into decisions about radiotherapy and adjuvant CDK4/6 inhibitors.

tentpoleSessions.title: Global Cancer Prevention: Current Approaches and Future Directions
tentpoleSessions.chairs: Isabelle Soerjomataram
tentpoleSessions.speakers: Isabelle Soerjomataram (International Agency for Research on Cancer); Karen Canfell, PhD, D.Phil (Cancer Council NSW); Clement Adebamowo, FACS, FWACS, MBChB, ScD (University of Maryland Marlene and Stewart Greenebaum Comprehensive Cancer Center); Panel Discussion (ASCO)
tentpoleSessions.panelists: Clement Adebamowo, FACS, FWACS, MBChB, ScD; Karen Canfell, PhD, D.Phil
tentpoleSessions.topics:
• Global Estimations of Cancer Attributable to Preventable Factors: Harnessing Data to Inform Prevention Policies
• Elimination of Vaccine-Preventable Cancers: Lessons From Global HPV-Prevention Strategies
• Evidence-Based Cancer Control in Africa: Advancing Prevention Through Research, Policy, and Capacity Building
• Panel Question and Answer
tentpoleSessions.overview: Cancer prevention is underused in global cancer control, despite clear links between modifiable risks (tobacco, obesity, alcohol, HPV, HBV) and cancer. Faculty will review the global burden of preventable cancers and highlight effective strategies to reduce these burdens across diverse health systems.

tentpoleSessions.title: Immunotherapy in Early-Stage Colorectal Cancer: Paradigm Shift in Cure and Nonoperative Management
tentpoleSessions.chairs: Christopher Lieu, MD, FASCO
tentpoleSessions.speakers: Kai-Keen Shiu, PhD, FRCP (University College London Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust); Stacey A. Cohen, MD (Fred Hutch Cancer Center, University of Washington); Christopher Lieu, MD, FASCO (University of Colorado, Anschutz School of Medicine); Panel Discussion (ASCO)
tentpoleSessions.panelists: Stacey A. Cohen, MD; Kai-Keen Shiu, PhD, FRCP
tentpoleSessions.topics: • Neoadjuvant vs Adjuvant Immunotherapy for Microsatellite Instability-High Colon Cancer • Is There a Role for Immunotherapy in Localized Microsatellite-Stable Colorectal Cancer? • Nonoperative Management After Neoadjuvant Immunotherapy in Microsatellite Instability-High Localized Colorectal Cancer—One Size Fits All? • Panel Question and Answer
tentpoleSessions.overview: Faculty will discuss the evidence and ongoing arears of investigation for the role of neoadjuvant and/or adjuvant immunotherapy for patients with early-stage colorectal cancer. Topics will include neoadjuvant vs. adjuvant immunotherapy, the role of immunotherapy for localized MSS colorectal cancer, and nonoperative treatment following neoadjuvant immunotherapy.

narratives.hostOpen: Welcome to ASCO Hype for Tuesday, June 2. Turn it up: this is the live conference desk, radio-DJ style, source-forward and moving fast.
